speaker: interviewer
text: --five. The survivor is Harriet Solz. And we're in Forest Hills, New York, USA.

speaker: interviewer
text: I need 15 seconds more and then we can start.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll start asking you questions again. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] I just want to ask you just a little bit more about the ghetto, if there's anything more that you remember about it. Do you remember-- were there Jewish police as well? Was it just the Germans, you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had Jewish police in ghetto. And, like, some were good, you know. They had to take orders from the Germans. But in the ghetto was Jewish. Outside the ghetto, the German police were staying, outside the gate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents frightened of the Jewish police? Do you remember any incidents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wouldn't say they were frightened. No, I wouldn't say that they were frightened. But my whole life got shattered, so, from such a beautiful, pampered life, to be exposed to brutality, murder. That's what I saw. That was my upbringing, you can say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does any specific incidence of brutality that you saw in the ghetto stand out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, the actions, how they ran through the houses pulling out people, beating them, and take them to the [NON-ENGLISH], to send them out to crematoriums. All those things, you saw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember for-- you talked a little bit about trying hiding in the ghetto. Do you remember any times like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hiding in the ghetto was one man who-- was a very big action, and one man-- one man saved us, hid us. Where, I don't remember even, but I know he hid us. That saved my sister and my life. Where we were hidden, some like a bunk-- I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were apart from your parents at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, at that time, apart from my parents. But like this, I was constantly with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's go back to the walk to Plaszow. How long was the walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Took a long time, because people were lined up like six people, I think, in a row. And we were walking to get to Plaszow. But we came out alive from ghetto, which our organization commemorates every year, the liquidation of Cracow Ghetto, because that was the biggest killing at that time. They were shooting at random at you. Whoever got hit got hit, for no reason at all. And of course, a lot of people would remain in Ghetto B. And they went straight to the ovens.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did you know about what was going on, the concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: You knew. There were rumors. People were talking. And people smuggled out children that time in, like I mentioned, suitcases, knapsacks. And under the coats, they carried small children. And they brought them to Plaszow. And they let them stay there for a while, but didn't last too long. They lined them up, all the children. But if we were lucky, we went to Schindler. Otherwise, we would be among those kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, when you were walking to Plaszow, was it cold outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was very cold. It was March 13. In Poland, that's very cold weather yet. It was cold, freezing. And I dragged my strainer. God forbid I should loose that strainer. And I had my pajamas, how will I sleep? So they put us in one big barrack, and I slept on my knapsack. Next day, I wake up, I have nothing. Somebody snatched the bag.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were on the walk, did-- were there are a lot of German guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, standing with the guns pointing towards you. You were afraid to breathe. In case you want to scratch your nose, they might think it's something else, that you try escape, or whatever, so you were petrified.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have dogs, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. They had dogs, especially Goth didn't budge without his dogs.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to Plaszow, what do you remember seeing when you-- when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was dark when we got to Plaszow. They put us in one barrack, everybody, and we slept on the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the registration process like when you first got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall that. I don't know. There was registration or not, I don't remember. But I know once we got-- the next day, I was separated from my mother. My mother, with my sister, went to one barrack, and I went with my cousin to another barrack. How and what, I don't know how this happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get to see your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the evenings after work. So I was assigned to work outside camp in a factory from [NON-ENGLISH], because we were making socks, supposed to  knit socks, the bottom of the socks, for German soldiers. They send in the uppers. They were already in Russia, the German soldiers, 1943. And they needed warm socks. So we were supposed to knit the bottoms of the socks. But the people found them-- made the machines-- they found machines that they were making the bottoms of the socks and look like handmade. And we just attached the bottom to the uppers. And you couldn't tell that they weren't handmade because how much they expect of you to make during the day, it was impossible to fill the contract.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did they expect you to make?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember, but it was very hard. And we were able to fill the contracts because they were done on the machine. That's the only way. And then, in Plaszow-- that was at the beginning-- I went to wash myself. Everything was outside, outdoors, the latrines-- you know, latrines, they were the washrooms with the toilet. And me, the delicate thing, caught pneumonia right away. And my cousin dragged me through the gates, because we were going to work. So we had to pass the gates of the Germans, who watched us with guns pointing to us, that I had 104 fever. And she grabbed me-- dragged me with other women, that they shouldn't notice I am sick. And over there, they tried, while I was sick, to put me in the corner to be able to lie down, people to help me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at night, my father-- I used to sneak into his barrack. And he put on me-- I don't know if you know about it-- [POLISH]. Did you ever hear about that? These are small glasses. And you warm them up with a spirit, and you put it on your back. And this pulls out the disease. And every night, he put those [POLISH] He saved my life, because there was a time I was so sick that I could not really go. I went to the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: And while I was in the hospital in Plaszow, the Jewish police came running. Whoever can leave the hospital, leave. I don't care how, but otherwise you're going to be killed. They're coming for inspection. And that's what happened. And I ran out, and I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still very sick when you ran?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still very sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you ran out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because, otherwise, I knew I'll be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many girls were in the barracks with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a lot. How many, I don't know. But you were lying on such a one long board next to each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you uniforms to wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute. Oh, the uniforms we had in-- oh, no, not in Plaszow, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were wearing your own clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we wore our own clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they give you to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little, very little. A dry piece of bread, sometimes a soup, which was like water. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel? What were your spirits like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mine, I was scared to death. I was afraid to breathe, because I was so frightened that you can't imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any incident in particular that you remember that was particularly frightening?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole life in Plaszow, this was like second Auschwitz to me, was frightening, was scary. Oh, we had an incident. We went to work, and we didn't finish the whole job. We were building a house. So they were going to kill us at night. So the official start running to Goth, asking to save our lives. So at the end, he saved our life. But we had to work all night long, stay in the water to our knees in the pounds, with shovels to pull out the mud. And Germans with the dogs were standing all around. We were supposed to be killed, but they saved us this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the official who saved your life Jewish or German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish used to get involved. And we're a whole group, maybe 40 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much exposure did you have to Amon Goth?

speaker: interviewee
text: To what?

speaker: interviewer
text: To Goth. How much exposure did you have to-- to the commandant?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Goth? I had no close contact with him, but every day he was checking us. They were counting us. We had to line up every morning to make sure everybody's here. And he used to inspect, walk through. If he passed you, you were lucky. But you didn't know if you're not being shot, because he enjoyed killing people. That was his sport. And you didn't know whom he's going to shoot next. The person next to you could have been shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a big camp. How, I don't know. I don't want to give you wrong information, because I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it all Jews who were there? Were there Gypsies? Were there Polish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, all Jews, all Jewish people, as far as I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came '43 March. And maybe July, we went to Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. But the whole family, we all went to Schindler in Emalia.

speaker: interviewer
text: The factory that he had there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we worked for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you got there, how many people went also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember, really. But it was considered very lucky that we got into Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the first time you saw him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was always around. Very handsome man, good-looking, tall, all the women were crazy about him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You felt you weren't scared of him. You felt at ease somehow, because there was no killing, no shooting. You had a number? Did you get a-- Yeah. We all-- by-- in Emalia, we got numbers-- not numbers, the letter K and L. This means, in German, concentration camp, [GERMAN], as that's what the order's to give us. And to me, a tattoo represented something awful. You can't imagine how I was affected by that tattoo. First of all, I remembered, from home, who had tattoos, The lowest class, the sailors. And I still had in me that, you know, that snobbish upbringing, you would say. I don't know. That tattoo did it to me. Then I said, in case I want to run away or what, I'll be marked for life with that K and L.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was ammunition-- there was ammunition factory, and we took pieces of iron which you fall off from-- when you cut iron, thin pieces falling off. I took this and I pulled out skin, the whole meat I had on my left wrist. I pulled that out, the whole meat where it says K and L.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had some infection. I don't need to tell you. But it disappeared. But if the Germans would have found out that-- a lot of people did it, not only me. If they would have found out we did this, they would have killed us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, if you were under Schindler's protection, why did you have to have the-- why did you get the numbers there in Emalia? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably they were orders from Goth, because we belonged to Goth, only we were the [GERMAN]. We had to wear the special uniforms, the striped uniforms, by Schindler too. So the orders must have come from Goth to make sure that no one runs away, because it was easier to run away than from Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in Plaszow, do you remember any mention of Schindler or his list, or people trying to get on the list?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. The list became first after being in Emalia. Before, there was no list. Only he had the factory with parts, enamel parts, and that's all what we knew. But once the Russians start coming closer-- so he built the camp in Brnenec, in Czechoslovakia, to save his Jews, what he called them, his Jews. And we knew we going to Brnenec. And this was-- that time, the list was full.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was later, though.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on. It was full.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had your parents met Schindler at all? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they first meet Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. But I mean, you were able to talk to him if you had to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him in Plaszow at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in Plaszow I didn't see him. Plaszow was a very big camp, a large camp. I don't know how many troops they had, but it was very big.

speaker: interviewee
text: And who watched us in Plaszow? They took from prisons, German prisons, killers, murderers. And they were our supervisors. We had women police women who were serving time in prisons in Germany. And I remember one German, they called them the orphan. Why? Because he killed his parents. And these were our supervisors who watched over us, real murderers. Were they very cruel to you? Do you remember specific instances?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried always to get lost somehow, personally. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was hit maybe once, twice. That's about it. I got away. Let's put it that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you hit by hand or with something?

speaker: interviewee
text: By hand, by hand. But I cannot say that I really-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] special contact, close, I didn't have it. I had it in Auschwitz, but not there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see people being punished in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. They neat-- they had to pull out their pants, and they beat them, whipped them, put down 25. They had to count it. If they made a mistake, they had to count again from the beginning, 25 with the [GERMAN]. This is out of leather made thing, you know, like you whip a horse, such a whips. And they were hitting you and pouring water on the wounds, which burned like hell. I saw hanging. I saw killing. They hanged people in Plaszow. And, also, you see, I almost forgot the most important thing. I work in Plaszow-- oh, this was when I came back from Schindler-- I worked with dead bodies. But I am skipping. I should wait till--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we'll definitely talk about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, remind me, because I don't remember lots of things. I worked with dead bodies. We were digging out the graves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, what were some of the offenses that would earn a whipping in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the offenses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. They didn't need an excuse. Or somebody said they stole a piece of bread or smuggled through a piece of bread. I mean, nothing big. Or there was a black market. Maybe they caught them selling. You didn't need an excuse, believe me, to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the day that you were taken to Emalia, do you remember anything about that at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really how it happened, but I know I opened my eyes there. I wasn't so frightened by Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was the journey, do you remember, from Plaszow to Emalia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wasn't so far. I don't remember if we walk or they drove us. I don't remember even, to be honest with you. But this was in Cracow. Where it was located, I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you living with your family again in Emalia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Men were separate, and women were separate. But there, I was with my mother and my sister in Emalia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And day to day, what was life like in Emalia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly, people work in his factory. I was helping in the kitchen peeling potatoes, which we had to fill up, you know, the pails with potato. And women screamed at me because I am not doing a good job, that my peels are too thick and we're losing out on the potato. And the people work in the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were able to eat better there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I, personally, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the first time that you saw Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think when we got there. I mean, he was always there on the grounds. He was always around. Did he talk to you? No, me? To me, he didn't talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: To your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe. With all the people, he would talk. But with me, what kind of business he had to talk to me?

speaker: interviewer
text: You said he saved your eyesight?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Brnenec.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll talk about that later.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he didn't speak to me personally, either. That's through other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your parents say to you about Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents? I don't recall they should talk at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you at the factory for, in Emalia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say a year, one year, which made me grow up a little bit. It made me older, more mature.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any schooling at all while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was finished in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about what was going on outside? Did you hear anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we all knew. We heard rumors. We thought that when we were in ghetto, the war ended. And because they said the Russians start the war against Germany, and we thought the war will be over any day. But in meanwhile, we didn't see any end to it. And always, we heard the Russians are getting closer, and the Germans trying to cover their-- what you call this, how to say it-- to cover their-- to cover what they did to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they doing to try to cover what they were doing to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Digging up graves, burning the bodies, which was part of my work. And the smell-- you can't imagine what the awful smell.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is when you were back in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: When back in Plaszow. They tried to cover up and to cover the graves. There shouldn't be any marks left of what they did to Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why you went back from Emalia back to Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went because the Russians were getting closer. And Schindler had to liquidate this camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what time of year it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the summer. I would say September, July, I don't know. But he had to liquidate Emalia. And he built a new camp in Czechoslovakia, called Brnenec, for his people, the ammunition factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: But first, you went back to Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Plaszow to wait for the transport. We were supposed to go from Plaszow straight to Brnenec. What happened in between, I really don't know. But we were in Plaszow for a nice few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you felt the day that you found out you had to go back to Plaszow after feeling safe?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were petrified. We were petrified, because, still, a few people remained in Emalia for the liquidation of the camp. And my father didn't know what to do, if we should go with the transport to Plaszow, which was hell on earth, or to be left to the liquidation of camp. But usually, when they were liquidating camps, they liquidate the people. So this was our choice, to go back to Plaszow. We had no other choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how many people were in Emalia at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: A thousand people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how many went back to my Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of them. We were 300 women, 700 men. So most of them went back to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember if you walked, or if it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't recall it. I don't recall if we walked or they took us by trucks. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went back this time, how did the camp seem different, Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was hell. Plaszow was unbelievable. It only was missing the crematoriums, the ovens. Otherwise, was hell on earth. It's very hard to describe it, even. All of it, the Germans with the dogs, and here is killing, there is killing. You really-- you didn't know if you were coming or going. And that time when we came to Plaszow, it just happened they had a transport going, for men, to Mauthausen. Wagons were waiting for men to ship them to Mauthausen. So they took our men, put them on the wagons to Mauthausen, which they never made Brnenec, because everybody knew Brnenec was going to be good, going to save your life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father in the transport to Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was. And it was so hot, and they had no water. I remember I was running towards the wagons to see him. It was terrible. And like you see in the Spielberg where he hoses down all those wagons with water, with cold water, this was that transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Schindler there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schindler came down to Plaszow. And he-- like in the movie, he shows, let's get them wet for a joke. But this cold water helped them. There was such a heat that it helped them a little bit in those wagons.
